data.title: Baldwin Group's CAC Purchase: Strategically Sound, But Debt Is Concerning
data.description:
Shares of The Baldwin Insurance Group, Inc. (BWIN) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing nearly half of their value. The company has struggled with an excessive valuation as margins have moderated, partially due to accounting changes. Even with the stock down substantially, Baldwin just announced an acquisition with a large equity component, suggesting that BWIN is still comfortable issuing shares here. With a major acquisition and shares down substantially, now is a good time to determine if Baldwin is attractive going forward.

With CAC, Baldwin Adds A High-Quality Business

On Dec. 2, Baldwin agreed to buy CAC Group for $1.03 billion. Baldwin is paying $438 million in cash and issuing 23.2 million shares, worth ~$589 million, and in 2030, there will be a deferred $70 million payment made. Depending on retention and performance, there could be an additional $250 million of payments. Baldwin expects to close this deal in Q1 2026, and I do not foresee any antitrust concerns.

CAC is the nation’s 35th largest insurance broker, while Baldwin is the 15th. Together, they are the 12th largest insurance broker, with ~$14 billion of annual premiums. This remains a fairly fragmented industry, with ongoing consolidation likely. With the company not even a top-5 player, regulatory pushback to this deal is unlikely to be material.

CAC has been a high-growth business. Since 2020, it has grown revenue by ~29% per year, and it has an extremely productive workforce. Its average colleague generates ~$481k in retained commissions and fees, more than $200k above the top quartile insurance broker. Importantly, its average advisor is much younger than the industry average, and at 46 years old, most of its advisors have meaningful careers ahead of them. That is an important consideration, as advisors nearing retirement may be highly productive today, but retaining those sales after retirement could prove difficult.

CAC has grown by adding advisors to sell insurance policies, as well as making these advisors more productive. In fact, 76% of advisors generate over $1 million in commissions once hitting the 3-year mark with CAC, up from 48% in 2021. Now, the biggest risk of a merger like this is that agents leave for other firms, taking much of their business with them. This is why there are $250 million of earn-outs, to insulate BWIN from higher attrition. Importantly, CAC is majority owned by its employees and advisors. With an ownership culture and a stake in the firm, the advisor base should be fairly sticky.

CAC has $310 million of revenue and ~$70 million of EBITDA. Baldwin expects to generate ~$60 million of synergies. Including run-rate synergies, Baldwin expects this transaction to increase EPS by ~20%. Management expects to achieve $10 million of synergies next year and fully realize synergies by 2028. 25% of synergies are tied to revenue, which I tend to view as more aspirational. Baldwin offers reinsurance brokerage solutions, and taking this Juniper Re offering and extending it to existing CAC clients does offer a clear path for revenue upside. Expense savings primarily come from removing duplicative headcount and functions (technology, office, etc.). I view ~$50 million as a more reasonable synergy base case, given my hesitancy to credit all revenue targets.

Debt Is Elevated

Over the past few years, Baldwin has been prioritizing getting leverage of 3-4x, and the company has made progress towards this with leverage just outside the high end of the range at 4.1x. With leverage approaching the target, Baldwin had announced an intention to authorize a $200 million buyback, but I expect repurchases to now be on hold. With ~$438 million in cash being paid at closing and CAC having ~$70 million of a standalone EBITDA, this will bring pro forma leverage towards 4.5x.

Now with $60 million of synergies, this deal would be slightly deleveraging if the synergies are fully realized, though there are also potential earnout payments. Importantly, Baldwin has no near-term maturities, with its first debt due in 2029. I expect leverage to end 2026 in the low 4x range, and I would not expect material buybacks before 2027. Overall, I view Baldwin’s financial policy as aggressive, but not dangerously so.

Overall, this deal adds scale, making Baldwin a more meaningful player in the insurance broker sector while giving it more exposure to niches like insurance products for natural resources and private equity. Additionally, Baldwin is acquiring a highly productive advisor base. That said, it leaves the company with elevated debt for longer, and buybacks are no longer likely to be a capital allocation focus. Maintaining CAC’s culture during the integration will also be essential.

Standalone Results Are Mixed

Aside from the CAC deal, Baldwin’s performance has been mixed. In the company’s third quarter, reported on Nov. 4, Baldwin earned $0.31 per share, which matched expectations, as revenue grew by 8% to $365 million. Organic revenue was up 5%, but EBITDA was flat at $73 million as the company has seen modest margin pressure. Margins were 19.8% in Q3, down 170bps from last year. The company’s backlog is sitting at a record high, and this should mostly be realized in H1 of 2026. Additionally, the company is undertaking a 3-year transformation program aimed at increasing automation. This program will cost $40 million to implement, with run-rate savings targeted at $50 million by the end of 2028.

Now, adjusted EBITDA and adjusted EPS exclude share-based compensation, which is a noncash expense. Baldwin is not unique in doing this, but I believe it is wrong to exclude this expense. While noncash, share-based comp is a real cost in the form of dilution. Share-based comp was $22 million in Q3 and stands at $52 million year to date. If investors add back share-based compensation, adjusted EPS for 2025 would be ~$0.30 below guided levels.

Sales velocity was a strong 20%, though this was down from 22% in Q2. Accounting changes have delayed when the company will recognize revenue until some policies expire, creating a ~$7 million headwind and reducing growth by about 2%. The company is seeing some pressure on policy pricing, particularly tied to catastrophe risk, which matches what reinsurance firms have been saying. Lower insurance pricing is a net headwind for Baldwin, as Baldwin earns a commission tied to the value of the policy. If a policy's price is lower, that mechanistically reduces its commission. We have seen insurance premium inflation steadily moderate, and I expect pricing increases of 0%-3% next year, down from a peak closer to 6%, which will be a headwind for revenue growth.

Adjusted free cash flow is $76 million year-to-date. This is closer to ~$24 million if we add back share-based compensation. Baldwin ended the quarter with $90 million of cash and $1.6 billion of debt. This will pass $2 billion of debt upon completion of the CAC deal. I expect the company to gradually work to bring debt down under $1.8 billion before buying back shares. Until buybacks begin, we will likely see the share count rise 2%-3% due to share-based compensation.

In Q4, Baldwin expects to earn $0.28-$0.32 on ~$350 million of revenue. Baldwin also offered preliminary 2026 guidance of $1.66-$1.7 billion of revenue and ~$1.95-$2.10 in EPS, with free cash flow up “double-digits.” This was before the CAC deal, which ultimately should add about $0.25-0.30 of EPS. These benefits will largely be after 2026, given the time it takes to generate synergies.

With Debt Reduction The Next Focus, Shares Are A "Hold"

With slowing premium inflation and integration costs, I expect about $1.70 in 2026 EPS (adding back the cost of share-based compensation) or about $2.00 excluding share-based compensation. Shares are trading at 15x, which is at a discount to larger peers like Willis Tower Watson (WTW), which trades closer to 18x forward earnings. That said, WTW has much larger scale as well as a strong balance sheet which enables capital returns. Baldwin has a need to focus on debt reduction, and its share count will steadily grow.

As a result, I struggle to see material multiple expansion. Owners of CAC shares who are receiving BWIN stock may also choose to sell shares now that their stake is substantially more liquid, a further headwind to multiple expansion. This acquisition is sound, but with further debt, BWIN’s financial position leaves me as a "Hold," especially with under $50 million of underlying free cash flow (ex-SBC), meaning that deleveraging will be a prolonged process.

data.title: Was It The Week That Wasn't? - Weekly Blog # 917
data.description:
Originally published on November 30, 2025

Does 3½ US Trading Days make a week?

The bullish media and "street" pundits were thrilled that the 3½-day trading week restored early November losses to the popular stock averages, although they were disappointed the rise did not break through to new highs. Looking at the results, they resembled a week from a younger bull market.

Reality may have been the problem

At least one analyst calculated that if you eliminated all “AI”-related activity since 2019, “the market” is probably down. This suggests that since 2019, we have experienced a slowly declining bear market. The Conference Board’s measure of confidence recently dropped to 88.7%, which was more than the expected reading of 93% and the prior reading of 95.5%. HP (HPQ), the old equipment producer part of Hewlett Packard, joined many other large employers in announcing expectations of a 10% job cut. The American Association of Individual Investors (AAII) sample survey for the last three weeks reported bullish projections of 32.0%, 32.6% and 31.6%, respectively, for the next six months. Their bearish projections remained in the 40-49% range.

Regular subscribers to these blogs have learned of my concerns about the declining quality of balance sheets, a warning sign of economic turmoil. One measure of this is the much larger growth in volume on the NASDAQ vs. the “Big Board”. In the short Friday trading session, the decline in volume on the NASDAQ was twice as large as the percentage decline on the NYSE.

Two Causes of Economic Turmoil

As with the run-up to the 1929 crash, the Roaring Twenties led to overconfidence (AI?) and unsound leverage (Private Capital?). The organizational hollowing-out is causing an increase in execution risk. Governments, universities, businesses, and families reacting to increasing financial strain are looking to improve efficiencies. Efficiency, not effectiveness, is measured by output versus input. Many have assigned revenues or other outputs to those at both the top and bottom of the production ladder. The people in the middle, mostly supervisors/middle management, have not been credited with the output assigned to those at the top and bottom and have been reduced or eliminated entirely. One glaring example is the federal government, although this trait is found throughout society. The President has had difficulty getting many of his actions approved by the courts. In numerous cases, there was insufficient careful staff work, which would have phrased efforts better or would have raised internal discussion instead of simple loyalty in attempting to execute flawed orders. This is a pattern exhibited in other organizations.

Thoughts?

Original Post

data.title: Veritone: AI Pipeline Grows, But Sales Execution Is The Open Question (Upgrade)
data.description:
Characterizing 2025 as the year of the AI gold rush probably wouldn't be an overstatement. This is the year where every company has been challenged to demonstrate how they are using AI or selling it in order to get acceptance in the public markets. And for software companies in particular, the race is on to prove which products can remain relevant in an AI age that will be dominated by agentic products.

Amid this backdrop, Veritone, Inc. (VERI) has seen a surprising resurgence. The company describes itself as the "AI operating system," and has evolved beyond its legacy ad-tech business to build up a new book of business in enterprise software. But with the stock up ~60% since the start of the year, we have to ask ourselves: is the stock market putting the cart before the horse in this small-cap, largely untested stock?

I last wrote a sell article on Veritone in October, when the stock was still trading in the high $5s. Since then, Veritone has dropped about ~10%, while the company has also released strong Q3 results that showcased improving consumption ARR and software revenue growth that suggests some level of success for its new AI platform. I'm upgrading Veritone to a neutral rating: though I think Veritone's return to revenue growth puts a floor on recent share price performance, Veritone still also has a choppy history for execution, which doesn't make this a comfortable long-term hold for me.

"AI Tokenization" strategy is yielding consumption ARR gains

In early December, Veritone hosted a conference in which the company laid out its vision for its AI product as well as its go-to-market approach. This acted as a major upside catalyst for the stock, driving a ~20% rally from the mid-$4s to the mid-$5s.

Veritone envisions its "AIware" operating system to be the layer that helps companies transform unstructured data into "tokens" that are able to be ingested by AI models for analysis and tasks. Unstructured data, as a reminder, is data that can't fit neatly into rows and columns like an Excel spreadsheet or traditional database: it's the video, image, and audio files that continue to see rapid generation and which businesses view as containing valuable insights.

Summarizing this vision during the conference, CEO Ryan Steelberg noted as follows:

Through VDR, we have established ourselves as a premier data and model training partner for many of the largest hyperscalers and foundational model developers. We transform, again, raw, unstructured audio and video into high-quality tokenized data sets that can be used to train and fine-tune the world's most advanced AI models.

VDR is a new product line that is being sold into both public sector and commercial enterprise customers. With a qualified near-term pipeline and bookings already surpassing $40 million, VDR is emerging as a critical partner to many global hyperscalers and the next generation of AI model developers.

As we've stated on previous calls, we are confident by the end of the year, we will hold active contracts or projects with nearly every major hyperscaler in the market, further solidifying VDR's position as a critical enabler in the unstructured AI training data ecosystem.

Our tokenization platform not only powers our AI workflows and customer applications today, but now serves as the foundation for a powerful new monetization framework. The data as a currency era has arrived and Veritone is uniquely positioned to capitalize."

The company further believes that the "tokenization" of data as an asset will become a $13.5 trillion industry by 2030. Now, Veritone has made claims about being an AI company long before the AI gold rush took off: but what makes me less pessimistic on Veritone now (versus my previous Sell rating) is that we're starting to see evidence of the company's success play out in its quarterly results.

The chart below breaks out Veritone's revenue in its most recent quarter by product and by customer segment.

Total revenue grew 33% y/y to $29.1 million, ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $28.4 million (+29% y/y) by a four-point margin. Veritone's software revenue, meanwhile, jumped 55% y/y to $22.8 million.

This is a massive turnaround from Q2, where Veritone's total revenue declined -2% y/y and software revenue grew just 12% y/y, hence my change in sentiment on this stock. Furthermore, we're also seeing a boost in ARR. The company's SaaS ARR was roughly flat at ~$50 million, but its consumption ARR jumped 54% sequentially to $18.8 million, which is a signal of healthy deal activity for its consumption pricing-based AI platform.

New bookings also rose 30% y/y to $21.5 million, which bodes well for further revenue acceleration. We're still concerned that the company is shedding total software customers: but as long as the company is landing higher-spending AI customers (which its ARR and bookings metrics seem to suggest), Veritone is still moving in the right direction.

CEO Steelberg noted that the VDR platform also has $40 million of qualified opportunity, representing roughly 2x the current quarter's bookings. Per his remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

Our qualified VDR pipeline and bookings now exceed $40 million, reflecting 100% growth quarter-over-quarter. Importantly, that figure represents only current bookings and near-term opportunities. Our total VDR pipeline now spans multiple sectors, regions and time horizons, positioning Veritone for sustained growth as AI developers increasingly move beyond open web data in favor of proprietary multimodality data sets, precisely the domain VDR was built to serve.

Looking forward, we are confident that by the end of 2025, Veritone will hold active contracts or projects with every major hyperscaler in the market."

Another positive to note: Veritone negotiated a debt paydown, and after the transaction it's in a far more benign liquidity position, with $34 million of cash and $45 million of debt, or just $11 million in net debt.

Valuation and key takeaways

At current share prices in the low $5s, Veritone trades at a market cap of $478.3 million; tacking on the $11 million of net debt on Veritone's balance sheet after its debt paydown gives us an enterprise value of $489.3 million.

For next year, FY26, Wall Street is expecting Veritone to generate $131.0 million in revenue, representing 19% y/y growth. This positions Veritone's valuation at 3.7x EV/FY26 revenue.

Software companies that are growing revenue at a mid/high teens pace tend to trade at a mid/high single-digit multiple of revenue. Examples include Atlassian (TEAM), Workday (WDAY), and AppFolio (APPF). Veritone trades even more cheaply than HubSpot (HUBS), which has been in the penalty box all year:

That discount, in my view, reflects choppy execution on Veritone's part. Key for investors to watch will be if the company can sustain its current sequential improvements in software revenue growth and consumption ARR, as well as evidence that it can convert its $40+ million VDR pipeline into new bookings. So far, Q3 results look like incredible progress; hence, my rating upgrade on this stock: though Veritone has exhibited very lumpy, unreliable results in the past.

I still prefer to remain on the sidelines here, though improving performance certainly piques my interest to watch VERI stock more closely for a potential buy opportunity.

data.title: Trevi Therapeutics: "Buy" Rating For Haduvio Treating Chronic Cough In IPF And Beyond
data.description:
I'm initiating coverage of Trevi Therapeutics (TRVI) with a "Buy" rating, and the reason why is because it is already ahead of the game in terms of getting its lead program into phase 3 testing. I'm talking about the development of Haduvio [oral nalbuphine ER] for the treatment of cough in patients with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis [IPF]. Consider that the company has already been able to achieve positive topline results from the phase 2b CORAL study and is expected to file a meeting request with the FDA in Q4 of 2025. This is a crucial inflection point because if talks go well, then the company should be able to initiate a phase 3 study using Haduvio for the treatment of IPF patients with chronic cough in the 1st half of 2026. Bottom line is that this program is important because it is an unmet need, as there is currently no FDA-approved therapy for a majority of these IPF patients with chronic cough.

What puts this company in an even better position is that its drug Haduvio has not only achieved positive results in the phase 2b CORAL study treating chronic cough in patients with IPF. It has also been able to evaluate the use of it successfully in the phase 2a RIVER study treating patients with refractory chronic cough [RCC]. The point is that this is the only company with an investigational drug that has been able to achieve positive phase 2 data in treating chronic cough both in IPF and RCC patients. In terms of the positive data from the RIVER study, this will now allow the company to initiate a phase 2b study targeting RCC patients in the 1st half of 2026.

One reason to go over this company would be the positive data it achieved from both of the phase 2 studies, treating chronic cough in both IPF and RCC patients, respectively. The second reason is because it just released its Q3 of 2025 financial results and earnings update, noting that it has an expected cash runway into 2028. The final reason, which I believe sets this biotech up well with the positive phase 2 data it had reported to date, is that there was speculation of a potential buyer having takeover interest of this company. It makes sense, because chronic coughing in IPF patients is an unmet medical need with no FDA-approved therapies. Plus, it has a differentiated mode of action compared to other drugs in having both central and peripheral targeting.

Positive Phase 2b Data Leads Way To Possible Phase 3 Study In 2026

As I noted above, the company was able to achieve positive results from its phase 2b CORAL study using its drug Haduvio [oral nalbuphine ER] for the treatment of chronic cough in patients with Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis [IPF]. Before going over the positive data that it obtained from that mid-stage trial, plus any catalysts to come about from this program, I believe it is first important to understand what this disorder actually is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being. Pulmonary Fibrosis is characterized as a progressive, chronic disease of the lungs that is a result of fibrosis. This type of fibrosis causes inflammation of the lungs, and it also makes it hard for a patient to breathe. The term "idiopathic" means it results due to an unknown cause. There are two scenarios here with respect to IPF, which is that there are no symptoms present at all at first. However, as this disease progresses, then there are a host of symptoms that these patients experience. What's even worse is that IPF actually can lead to two other issues, which are:

  • Pulmonary Hypertension - blood pressure of the lungs increases to a higher than expected level
  • Respiratory failure - the lungs are unable to pump out enough oxygen to the brain and other organs leading to this type of failure

Currently available therapies aren't really that great. Why is that? They help, of course, but they only slow down the scarring [fibrosis] of the lungs. They are not actually cures for IPF. One drug approved to slow down disease progression is Esbriet [pirfenidone] from Legacy Pharma with U.S. rights and Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) with rights outside of the United States. The other two drugs are Ofev [nintedanib] and Jascayd [nerandomilast] from Boehringer Ingelheim.

Okay, but where does a drug from Trevi Therapeutics fit in? The company's goal is to alleviate a major issue associated with IPF and Interstitial Lung Disease [ILD], which is coughing. Consider that with approximately 150,000 IPF patients in the United States, approximately 85% of them are plagued with chronic coughing [Trevi Therapeutics Corporate Presentation November 2025, slide #6]. Not only does it interfere in their daily activities, but the coughing itself exacerbates the problem of disease progression. Chronic cough leads to further damage of the lungs. This is where Haduvio from the company comes into play, where it targets the entire cough reflex arc pathway. Specifically, it targets this pathway both centrally and peripherally. How so? By targeting opioid receptors through different aspects, which are Kappa agonists and Mu antagonists. This occurs with the following:

  • Kappa agonism - activating receptors of the central nervous system [CNS] and peripheral nerves - in turn reducing cough frequency
  • Mu receptor agonist - to reduce cough

Both kappa agonism and mu antagonism have the effect of reducing cough, but both are put together to reduce cough without activating the mu-opioid receptors [mu-opioid receptors are known to cause sedation and abuse potential]. The "central" brain of it reduced chronic cough hypersensitization [need to react to coughing] and then the "peripheral" limits signals from the lungs to the brain. As you can see, this company has a unique mechanism of action [MOA] of acting both centrally and peripherally. Only "peripheral" targeting has failed in chronic cough for IPF patients [Trevi Therapeutics Corporate Presentation November 2025, slide #7]. Those drugs that are only "fibrotic" targeting have not shown a benefit towards improving a patient's cough. With no FDA-approved therapies and current options being limited in potential, there is room for Trevi to target a majority of IPF patients that have chronic cough. The company is focused on reducing chronic cough for IPF, but the beauty is that it could possibly eventually expand the population it could go after. Besides IPF, there are non-IPF patients with Interstitial Lung Disease [ILD] who might benefit from treatment with Haduvio. Uncontrolled cough is observed in 50% to 60% of non-IPF ILD patients. Thus, the point is that this is an expansion opportunity that it could shift to, should it achieve great success in a potential phase 3 study.

In order to determine whether or not Haduvio would be effective in reducing cough frequency in patients with IPF, the company ran the phase 2b CORAL study. This trial was split up into three parts as follows:

  • 2-week titration period
  • 4-week double-blind fixed-dose period
  • 2-week follow-up period

The primary endpoint was to evaluate the relative change from baseline in 24-hour cough frequency versus placebo at Week-6. The truth is that the drug ended up being ideal for all of the doses tested. Specifically, during the double-blind fixed-dosed period [4-weeks] patients were randomized to receive one of the following doses:

  • 27 mg BID [twice-daily] dosing of Haduvio
  • 54 mg BID dosing of Haduvio
  • 108 mg BID dosing of Haduvio
  • Placebo BID dosing

All doses tested achieved a statistically significant [CHEST 2025 Annual Meeting Results Phase 2b CORAL Study, slide #6] relative change from baseline of drug versus placebo in terms of 24-hour cough frequency at Week-6. The highest dose achieved the best placebo-adjusted reduction of -43.3% [-60.2% for 108 mg of Haduvio and then -16.9% for placebo].

Another positive to consider is that Trevi did a drug-drug interaction study in healthy adult participants. Why would it run a study of this caliber? The whole point was to see that if it combined Haduvio with standard of care [SOC] antifibrotic drugs, there would be no systemic issues or negative reaction. With no negative pharmacokinetic [PK] findings observed, this was another positive development for this company. The whole point of bringing up this biotech and this specific program of developing this drug as a chronic cough treatment for patients with IPF is that there are two major catalysts to keep an eye on within a 1-year period. One catalyst of which is that the company is gearing up to request an end-of-phase 2 [EOP2] meeting with the FDA in Q4 of 2025 to discuss the next stage of clinical development for this program. The second catalyst, of which, if there is a general consensus of the FDA meeting, would be the actual initiation of the phase 3 study using Haduvio for chronic cough patients with IPF in the 1st half of 2026.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC filing, Trevi Therapeutics had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $194.9 million as of September 30, 2025. The reason for the cash on hand was because of an offering that the company did back on June 5, 2025. That is when it issued and sold 17,400,000 shares of its common stock at an offering price of $5.75 per share. In addition, it gave the underwriters a 30-day option to exercise up to an additional 2,610,000 shares of common stock at this same public offering price. The underwriters did indeed exercise their option in full; with that being said, the total aggregate gross proceeds from this offering were $115.1 million. The truth is that this company is in excellent shape in terms of cash. That's because the cash runway projection it laid out is that the funds it has will be enough to operate its business into 2028. The company burns roughly $13.9 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $10.1 million in R&D expenses and then $3.8 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Trevi Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the request for the FDA EOP2 meeting in Q4 of 2025. The risk here is that even though the company attained statistically significant results from the phase 2b CORAL study using Haduvio for chronic cough of patients with IPF, there is no assurance that the FDA is going to greenlight or allow the initiation of a phase 3 study. Even if a late-stage study is allowed for this indication, upon completion of it, there is no assurance that the primary endpoint is going to end up being met in a statistically significant manner.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the company evaluating the use of Haduvio for another chronic cough condition. This would be its program of using this drug to treat patients with refractory chronic cough [RCC]. The intention of Trevi is that it wants to initiate a phase 2b study treating these chronic cough patients. The risk here is that even though the company achieved positive results from its phase 2a RIVER trial for this program, there is no guarantee that it will achieve a similar or superior outcome in the potential initiation of the phase 2b study targeting these patients with RCC using Haduvio.

The third risk to consider would be in terms of a possible expansion opportunity. By this, I'm talking about the company potentially shifting over to evaluate the use of Haduvio for the approximately 50%-60% of 228,000 U.S. patients with uncontrolled chronic cough who have non-IPF ILD. The company states in its 10-Q SEC filing [page 21] that it intends to start a clinical trial for these non-IPF ILD patients who both have chronic cough and underlying fibrosis. The risk here is that even if it initiates a trial of this caliber, there is no assurance that Haduvio is going to achieve a positive outcome from this trial. That is, it is not guaranteed that it will work to reduce cough frequency or somehow improve fibrosis for these patients.

Conclusion

Trevi Therapeutics is already set up for long-term success. Of course, it still has a lot that it needs to do. For instance, it truly needs to complete a phase 3 program so that it could eventually obtain FDA approval for its drug Haduvio. Speaking of which, this is where the two catalysts I noted above come in. The first of which is the EOP2 meeting with the FDA, expected to be requested in Q4 of 2025. Should that meeting go well, then the company is going to be able to initiate a phase 3 study using Haduvio for the treatment of chronic cough in IPF patients. The good thing about this company is its alternate approach to treating chronic cough. That is its unique MOA of targeting the cough reflex arc, both centrally and peripherally.

In addition, it is already in a good position in terms of expanding towards other indications with this very same drug. This would be in terms of reducing chronic cough in both non-IPF ILD and RCC patients. What also really sets this company up well is that there are no FDA-approved therapies for reducing chronic cough in IPF patients. This adds to the speculation that there might be a takeover interest of this company by another due to the prospects that it has in place. With the potential to initiate a phase 3 study for Haduvio for the treatment of chronic cough in IPF patients in 2026, plus the ability to move towards a phase 2b study of the very same drug targeting non-IPF ILD patients, I believe that this stock should be initiated with a "Buy" rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nike Before Q2: The Turnaround Story Everyone Wants, But Still Can't Quite See
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I wrote about Nike, Inc. (NKE) was at the end of June, when they reported their fiscal Q4 and full-year results. Back then, the whole narrative was very much about whether we were at the very beginning of a turnaround or just looking at a temporary pause in a pretty ugly downtrend. I was cautiously optimistic in that piece, but I was also very clear that while I saw some early indications that the turnaround might start happening, I did not see enough to say “this is it, the turnaround is here.” It felt much more like Nike had had a really, really bad couple of years where a lot of internal mistakes collided with external headwinds at exactly the wrong time, and maybe, just maybe, they were finally done falling and starting to operate better and fix some of what they broke.

Because of all the risks I discussed back then - execution, tariffs, still high valuation - I said I would stay on the sidelines, and that while the possibility of a turnaround was there, I couldn’t honestly say the turnaround was already happening.

Now we’re a few weeks away from fiscal Q2 results on December 18th. I completely skipped fiscal Q1 in terms of proper coverage, and this makes it an interesting moment to come back to the name and see where things stand. This is essentially an earnings preview for Q2, with the benefit of hindsight on Q1 and a fresh comparison to what I wrote in June.

Before writing this piece, I went back and reread my previous article, and I have to say it was very interesting to compare what I said then to what the company did over the past few months. From a performance standpoint, if we look at the stock since that late June article, Nike has delivered a total return of about minus 7.5%, including the dividend paid, but not reinvested, while the S&P 500 (SP500) is up almost 11% over the same period. So, purely from a portfolio perspective, I am pretty happy with my Hold rating, and I would definitely say that staying on the sidelines back then was the right call.

Recent Headlines

The more interesting question is what is happening right now because Nike actually managed to surprise me with some of the recent headlines. First, they raised the dividend not so long ago, which is always a deliberate signal from management about confidence and long-term cash generation.

Second, they announced a partnership with Skims, and as a female Gen Z representative, I know exactly how huge that is and how big Skims is in terms of demand and brand power. Skims already has massive sales, and I remember how, a year and a half ago in New York, I was desperately trying to find a physical store to buy something from them, only to discover that it wasn’t open yet. I never managed to buy their products, even though I really wanted to.

The campaigns Skims runs on social media are incredibly powerful, and even if you completely ignore the Kardashian angle (and in my case, you can because I’m not from the U.S., I’m not a fan, I never watched their show, and I have no idea who they are and what they do), I still know a lot about Skims as a brand. I have seen their products and promotions so many times on social media that it’s obvious why this business is doing so well. Some of the products look genuinely thoughtful for women, and you can clearly see there is a reason behind the growth. So when Nike announced a partnership with Skims, that definitely caught my attention in a good way.

At the same time, alongside these “nice” headlines, Nike is also reshuffling its senior leadership in a pretty dramatic way. We have a new COO (this position did not exist before), they eliminated the CTO and CCO roles, and as much as this all fits into the turnaround narrative and sends a signal to the market that Nike is “trying,” my opinion on these moves is mixed.

On one hand, this absolutely supports the idea that management is not asleep at the wheel - they are clearly aware of the problems, they are taking visible steps, and they do want investors to see that they are changing things that didn’t work. On every earnings call recently, they’ve repeated that they fully understand the issues and are actively working to fix them, and the dividend increase plus leadership shake-up is consistent with that message.

On the other hand, if I look at the concrete details of what they’ve done with the org chart, it doesn’t look straightforward to me at all. I can’t tell you that it all makes perfect sense.

The company has created a new COO, and into that role they moved their Chief Supply Chain Officer, Venkatesh Alagirisamy. In his new position, he is supposed to lead technology as well as everything he already handled before, including supply chain, planning, operations, manufacturing, and sustainability, and by doing that, he is supposed to ensure that technology is fully integrated across the company. But if we really think about this, it’s not that he suddenly jumped several levels in responsibility. He was already a C-level executive, and now he’s a C-level executive with a slightly different title and more things to do. He basically went from CSCO to COO.

The more confusing part is that, under this reshuffling, they eliminated the role of Chief Technology Officer altogether, and the CTO left. As someone who is very interested in everything related to digital, technology, and the specific problems Nike has had in this area, I find this decision very hard to interpret without more explanation. I focused heavily on their digital strategy in my previous article precisely because it was such a weak spot, and I honestly do not see, at least for now, how putting technology under a former supply chain head instead of having a dedicated CTO will help.

So for now, without more detailed commentary from management, I cannot say this leadership shuffle will do a lot of good. On paper, it really sounds like the management wants to show that they are working in the right direction and that they are “integrating” things. The phrase that he will be ensuring that technology is fully integrated across the company immediately makes me wonder what the previous CTO was doing and whether this heavy digital underperformance is partly the result of technology not having the right leadership earlier.

In other words, at this point, I have many more questions than answers. It could absolutely turn out to be a positive change over time, but investors should understand that right now it looks more like a move to reassure investors that things are moving in the right direction.

Financials

I think it’s useful to go back to what I said I would be watching after Q4 and to check where things landed after Q1 because that gives us the context we need going into Q2. In the June article, I explained in detail how Nike got to where it is now. If you are not familiar with the story, you can go back to that piece and read the section “What Went Wrong Before This Quarter,” because I break it down there very thoroughly.

The short version is that Nike made a series of pretty bad internal decisions at the same time as the U.S. market was going through tougher import policies, rising cost pressures, and China-related setbacks. Their push into digital didn’t work at the scale they needed, but digital was becoming a growth engine for many other companies, so Nike was losing that battle.

Back then, I said that some of the changes we saw in Q4 looked like early progress and could point to a potential turnaround in the future. The specific metrics I wanted to watch were margins, digital performance, wholesale recovery, inventory trends, and SG&A expenses.

On margins, I said I wanted to see stabilization or eventual improvement. We are not there yet. If anything, what we’ve seen so far is stabilization of the decline, not stabilization at a healthy level. In Q1, gross margin decreased 320 basis points to 42.2%, and if you look at the guidance for Q2, the company expects margins to decline a further 300 to 375 basis points, including a 175 basis-point headwind from new tariffs. So you can argue it’s not getting incrementally worse quarter after quarter, so that’s some form of stabilization, but obviously, what I would like to see is margins stabilizing at a respectable level.

We expect Q2 gross margins to be down approximately 300 to 375 basis points including a net headwind of 175 basis points from the new incremental tariffs.

On digital, I said it was a very weak spot back in the summer, but in Q1, it did not just stay weak, it pretty much collapsed. Nike Digital was down 12% in the first quarter, which is worse than I personally expected. Wholesale, on the other hand, grew 7% and clearly was the star of the show, which is exactly the directional improvement I highlighted as likely. The market expected revenues to decline mid-single digits, but instead revenue grew 1%. That was a positive surprise and helped the stock, but it doesn’t change the fact that the quality of that growth is very mixed.

Inventory is an area where things are slowly moving in the right direction. In Q4, inventory started to improve, and in Q1, inventories were down 2% year-over-year. That is a major green flag, and if they can continue to trim inventory, it will be one of the most important building blocks for a healthier Nike. So, looking at my expectations back in time, I said Q1 could be disappointing, especially on revenue, and I thought the topline would be weaker, but instead, revenue surprised to the upside. Although margins shrank. Taken together, it still doesn’t look like a company in the middle of a turnaround.

One of the more interesting details is SG&A. In Q4, I was worried that they might overspend as they tried to repair the brand, and I said we could see SG&A rising as they poured money into marketing and “fixing things.” Instead, in Q1, SG&A declined. That naturally raises the question of whether they are deliberately underspending to make the numbers look better in the short term and support the turnaround narrative, or whether they are genuinely becoming more disciplined and efficient. Obviously, the second option would be much better, but the first one is not out of the question either.

Guidance

If you listen to the Q1 call, the message from management is pretty similar to what we heard in Q4. They say that they are aware of the issues:

Nike's journey back to greatness has only just begun. There is significant work ahead, especially in the areas of Sportswear, Greater China and Nike Direct. And as I've said to the team, progress won't be perfectly linear, but the direction is.

Management says they believe they can return to double-digit margins, and when they talk about double-digit margins, they refer to operating margins. Historically, Nike did sit comfortably in the range of around 11–14% on average, but over the last two years, operating margins have dropped to about 7%. The company insists it is possible to get back to double digits and points to their own history to justify that view, basically saying that with the right mix of business, better product and channel mix, the right geography and segment balance, they should be able to lift margins again. Their plan is to do that by reigniting organic growth, improving the full-price mix, and eventually benefiting from operating leverage as the business grows.

Directionally, this all sounds great. Every investor would love to see operating margins back in the low teens with solid organic growth and a cleaner digital business. But at the same time, no one should feel bad for asking whether this is realistic given how things have looked in the last few quarters, why the company wasn’t able to maintain those margins before, and what exactly will change now to make this time different. It’s one thing to say “we believe we can do it,” and completely another thing to show the execution.

For Q2, Nike expects revenues to be down low single digits, essentially the same kind of guidance they gave for Q1, which they then beat. Margins, as mentioned, are expected to decline by 300–375 basis points, including the 175-basis-point headwind from incremental tariffs. The tax rate for Q2 and the full year is expected to be in the low 20% range.

They say momentum with wholesale is strong, and as a result, they expect wholesale revenue to return to modest growth for fiscal 2026. That part looks believable and very helpful for the turnaround story.

At the same time, they do not expect Nike Direct to return to growth in fiscal 2026, even though they talk a lot about initiatives to boost that part of the business. SG&A is expected to grow low single digits for the year, which again tells you that they are not planning some wild spending spree to fix everything overnight, but also that the path to higher margins will not be driven by a massive cut in investments either. It’s a slow, grinding process.

Valuation & Conclusion

The last piece of the puzzle is valuation, and this is where my issues with this company become even heavier. Nike is still very expensive, and the fact that the stock jumped 20% on a “better than expected” earnings report that was not good fundamentally says a lot about how hungry the market is for a feel-good turnaround story here.

The same thing is happening now on a smaller scale - the stock rises on management changes and partnership announcements that look more cosmetic (at least for now) than transformational at this stage. The market clearly wants to believe in a clean turnaround and is ready to buy if the company starts delivering numbers that support that narrative. If Nike genuinely starts showing real turnaround in the numbers - not just small revenue beats with shrinking margins and collapsing digital, but real margin stabilization, digital recovering, China improving - I think investors will absolutely buy the stock even at a rich valuation.

For me, this creates a very weird situation. Unless the stock drops meaningfully from current levels, I don’t see myself buying it, even though I’m not arguing that it should trade right in line with the sector. You can absolutely make the case that Nike deserves a premium because the dividend is there and has been increased, the brand is incredibly strong, they are not heavily leveraged, they have plenty of cash, and, well, it’s Nike.

But still, with the way the numbers look today, I would need either a much more convincing turnaround in the fundamentals (or even a straightforward growth with a nice slope showing it won’t go away), or a much lower entry point to get interested. Realistically, I would be looking for another 20–30% downside from here, combined with evidence that the turnaround is actually happening, not just talked about on calls. Without that, I don’t see a compelling setup for me personally.

In my opinion, the stock can go down even further based on valuation alone, especially if the actual results fail to support the bullish narrative and the optimism coming from management, but I don’t expect it to go as low as I would like it to go to be attractive. So just like in June, I will be staying aside and following the Nike story, it’s for sure an interesting one.

data.title: Strive: Bitcoin Treasury - Opportunity Or Trap?
data.description:
I rate Strive Asset Management, LLC (NASDAQ:ASST) a Strong Sell, for serious investors looking to avoid speculative stocks with no earnings or profits. The company promotes itself as a Bitcoin treasury manager with the added positioning of highly political rhetoric including pushback against such themes as ESG and Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion. Its promotion of capitalistic philosophy is at odds with the facts, given the ultra-low stock price, lack of profits and level of short interest.

Q3 financial results were horrific in my view. Not only did the company declare an expected significant loss, but it also announced it would buy Semler Scientific to get into the preventative healthcare space, raising serious questions about its business model.

The question becomes is this company going to continue to burn cash or does it provide an opportunity for the current investor to snap up shares for a discount, hoping for a turnaround? The Trump administration has been more friendly to the Bitcoin industry due to donations, so some optimism behind its chances has some basis. The conclusion is this is a highly speculative stock, which mixes Bitcoin, politics, and young executives with bold visions of capitalism and fueling portfolios with crypto...and medical devices.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock are:

  • Stock is down -85% in the last 6 months, trading at $1.04 per share.
  • Does not currently pay a dividend.
  • Loses money, has no earnings.
  • Has a short interest of 15.19% indicating investors are betting against it, in my opinion.
  • Originally founded by Vivek Ramaswamy, the former Presidential candidate, who resigned a year later.
  • Bitcoin hit an all-time high of $126,000 on October 6 but has since dropped back down to $91,975.
  • Q3 declared a GAAP net loss of -$192.3 million.
  • Q3 declared an acquisition of a scientific company to get into the preventative healthcare space.

Seeking Alpha Quant does not cover the stock.

The chart below captures the story fairly well in my view. A decline of -94% since inception, $856 million in market capitalization, with a stock price of $1.04. In my experience, stocks under $5 tend to be in crisis, such as when Lehman Brothers went bankrupt during the Global Financial Crisis, and yet, some investors continued to buy the stock based on the optimistic view it still held value.

Bitcoin Treasury Management

Strive was founded in 2022 by Vivek Ramaswamy (former Presidential candidate) and Anson Frericks. It is headquartered in Dallas, Texas. In 2023 Ramaswamy resigned from the company to pursue his campaign for president, and two years later it went public.

ASST positions itself as the first publicly traded asset management firm to use a Bitcoin treasury strategy amplified with perpetual preferred equity. Its Vision is to integrate Bitcoin into retail and institutional portfolios, expanding from ETFs to Bitcoin Bonds. The company also plans to capitalize on retirement portfolio’s where recently a Trump executive order allows investors to invest in alternative investments such as Bitcoin in their retirement accounts.

Within their portfolio, the company offers preferred shares under symbol (SATA), with a 12% coupon rate, yielding 14.8% at today’s pricing.

Q3 Financial Results

On November 14, the company declared Q3 results, with the following financial highlights:

  • GAAP Net loss of -$192.3 million, or -$0.22 per share.
  • Projected loss of single digit million-dollar loss to a single digit million-dollar income in 2026.
  • Owns 5,886 Bitcoin, with a total cost of $683 million and fair value of $672.9 million as of 9/30/25.
  • Announced an acquisition of Semler Scientific in an all-stock transaction, which would make it a wholly owned subsidiary.

According to the press release, the acquisition of a medical device company will be a good fit with a Bitcoin company:

We also entered into an agreement and plan of merger to acquire Semler Scientific, Inc., which we expect will be accretive to the Bitcoin exposure to our shareholders and provide significant potential future value based on the synergy of Semler's medical device business and the expertise of Strive management and board members.

Overall, I have never seen such a collection of red flags to be wary of. In my experience, the management of this company is over confident and doesn’t understand portfolio strategy. The mixture of Bitcoin and medical devices doesn’t make any sense, even if one were to believe the board members were talented individuals.

Under Performance To Date

An investment of $10,000 in ASST since inception in 2022, would be worth $586 today, that’s a loss of -$9,414.

Valuation

ASST has an enterprise value of $750.8 million, with a market capitalization of $856.3 million. Total debt is $3.6 million, while cash is $109.07 million. The company does not have a price/earnings ratio because it has no earnings.

Outlook

ASST management declared 2026 would either lose just over -$1 million or create an income in the “single digit million dollar” area. In my view, this statement highlights the speculative nature of the investment. It's like flipping a coin, it could be good or really bad. While it is true that Bitcoin can be volatile, it doesn't inspire confidence in senior management, in my opinion.

Risks To Rating

ASST is a high-risk speculative stock. To date, the company has only declared losses, with the stock price reflecting a boom and bust of optimism and fear behind the company’s potential. The extreme volatility favors the bold trader, who is willing to take significant risks, but it does not demonstrate a company valued on its fundamentals.

Other risks include its business model, which is currently unproven. ASST places a large emphasis on holding and acquiring Bitcoin assets, which as an asset class itself is still rather new and volatile. In my view, Bitcoin pricing is driven by investor sentiment, going up in value during positive sentiment, then dropping at elevator speed upon bad news.

Reputation risk is another factor. As large institutions choose suppliers for pension and retirement portfolios, the question becomes how ASST will distinguish themselves among such large industry sponsors such as BlackRock, Fidelity, and Brookfield Asset Management.

Bottom Line

I rate Strive Asset Management, LLC – ASST as a Strong Sell, cautioning all investors that it is a highly speculative stock. The recently released Q3 financial highlights showed a significant loss and plans for this asset manager to buy a medical device company, which highlights their lack of a solid strategy.

Within the company’s history and communication, it emphasizes political views with a focus on capitalism yet isn’t a role model. In fact, the only people who seem to be making money are the executives who were paid “share-based compensation expense” despite the loss. Originally founded by Vivek Ramaswamy and Anson Frericks in 2022, Vivek resigned a year later in 2023 to run for President.

ASST is down in price -94% since inception. The stock trades at just over a dollar ($1.04). It doesn’t pay a dividend or have any earnings. The short interest is currently 15.19% indicating a group of investors are betting heavily against it.

Bottom line, ASST is a coin flip, in my view. It could be driven higher by Bitcoin enthusiasm or be forced to sell off its assets to get out of the business.

data.title: Palantir Q3 Earnings: Rule Of 40 Score Accelerates
data.description:
PLTR stock: Q3 earnings measured by the rule of 40

I last analyzed Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) on Oct. 23 with an article titled “Palantir Q3 Earnings Preview: Rethink Its DOD Reliance.” That article served as a preview for its Q3 earnings report (ER), in which I focused on PLTR's first major UK contract with the Ministry of Defence. I considered this a key milestone marking its diversification beyond U.S. government contracts and rated the stock as a hold.

Since then, the company has reported its actual earnings for Q3 on Nov. 3, 2025, as shown in the snapshot below. Therefore, this article’s goal is to examine the updates provided in Q3 and also look forward to its growth and profitability metrics in Q4. Given the high growth rate of its business, I find it very fitting to frame this examination by the rule of 40 (which will be referred to as R40 hereafter). In a nutshell, I see signs of a better/stronger combination of the company’s growth and profitability ahead, which leads me to reiterate my hold rating despite the expensive valuation.

Let me start with its Q3 ER. As seen in the next chart, PLTR delivered another terrific quarter, with all earnings metrics beating consensus. In particular, total revenue reached $1.18 billion, translating into a YOY growth of about 62.8%. Such a growth rate itself is already sufficient to push its R40 score above the threshold.

For readers new to R40, the following quotes from Wall Street Prep should provide the necessary background for this article:

The management teams of high-growth SaaS startups are often required to choose between rapid growth and expansion, or improving profitability – for which, the Rule of 40 has become a practical framework to balance the two concepts. The Rule – popularized by Brad Feld – states that a SaaS company’s revenue growth rate plus profit margin should be equal to or exceed 40%. Thus, the rule measures the balance between a SaaS company’s growth rate and profitability. Profitability can be measured by net profit, EBIT, or even EBITDA margin.

With this background, the next chart shows PLTR’s R40 score in the past 5 years since 2021 to provide a broader context. To wit, in this chart, the R40 score is defined as the summation of PLTR’s quarterly YOY growth rates of its sales and its net profit margin. As seen, PLTR’s R40 score has been on an overall upward trend. It pushed above the 40 threshold around 2024 and has been accelerating since then.

Next, I will explain why its R40 score will remain at a robust level or even further expand in the near future.

PLTR stock: Profit margin and growth outlook

Looking ahead to Q4 2025, the company is expected to release the ER on Feb. 2, 2025. If you recall from the first chart in the foregoing sector, consensus projection points to a total revenue of $1.34 billion, translating into a YOY growth of about 62.0% compared to the same period in FY 2024 (of around $827 million). Once again, the revenue growth rate itself is enough to push its R40 score well above the threshold.

To make the scorecard even stronger, PLTR’s profitability metrics are also on an expanding trajectory. As aforementioned, the application of R40 involves a certain degree of ambiguity in the choice of a profitability measure. In most cases, the further downstream of the income statement and/or cash flow statement we go (e.g., from EBITDA margin, to EBIT margin, to net profit margin, and finally free cash flow margin), the more stringent the rule becomes.

PLTR’s is by no means the “normal” case, as you can easily tell from the next chart. As seen below, its EBIT and EBITDA margins are about the same (21.8% vs. 22.5%), and both are noticeably below its net profit margin of 28.1%. This is due to PLTR’s superb scalability in my view, and I will revisit this point later. Also note how rapidly these margins have expanded in recent years. As a reflection, the 5-year averages of all these margins are negative, as seen. For Q4, based on consensus’ revenue estimate of $1.34 billion, EPS estimate of $0.23 on a normalized basis, and its current share count, the implied net margin would be 43.8%. When combined with the projected YOY revenue growth rate of 62.0%, the R40 score would be again over 100.

PLTR stock’s valuation in focus

As a downside, the terrific combination of growth and profitability comes with a hefty price tag. As you can see from the following chart, Palantir's valuation metrics collectively receive an off-putting F grade. The P/E ratios for Palantir are 266 on a TTM non-GAAP basis and 235 on a forward basis. Even the EV/Sales (on a TTM basis) is in the triple digits (102x).

These valuation metrics are not only extreme by absolute standards (say compared to the sector average of its own historical averages), but are also extreme when compared to other businesses that also pass the R40. As an example, the following McKinsey study provided statistics for the EV/sales ratio for a collection of businesses with passing R40 scores:

Data show that investors reward companies that are at or above the Rule of 40 with consistently higher enterprise value (EV) to revenue multiples. Moreover, the higher the number, the greater the gain. Top-quartile SaaS companies generate nearly three times the multiples of those in the bottom (see exhibit).

The detailed results from this report are shown in the chart below. As seen, scalable SaaS companies that can pass the R40 have enjoyed an average valuation multiple of 12x EV/sales. For those with a superbly scalable model with an R40 score in the top quartile, they were traded at a further premium of 22x EV/sales. By either standard, PLTR’s current EV/sales ratios (in the range of ~90x to ~100x on an FWD and TTM basis, respectively) are quite extreme.

Other risks and final thoughts

Back to the positives, if you recall from the data presented in a foregoing section, PLTR’s EBIT and EBITDA margins are both noticeably below its net profit margin. Furthermore, its net profit margin of 28.1% is, in turn, lower than its FCF (free cash flow) margin of 30.3%. This abnormality is a reflection of PLTR’s superbly scalable operation in my model.

To analyze a high-growth stock, investors should delineate the company’s growth CAPEX and maintenance CAPEX, which are both treated as expenses in accounting terms. For PLTR, the fact that its FCF margin is above than its net margin (which in turn is above its EBIT and EBITDA margins) is a sign that its accounting profits underestimate its true economic profits. Judging by the updates provided in Q3’s ER, I see several initiatives that could further strengthen its scalability.

In particular, I am impressed by the company’s ability to scale its AI FDEs (Forward Deployed Engineers). I consider its deep FDE expertise and Ontology implementation to be a crucial—and also winning—differentiator when compared against other competing products, which mostly provide generic and one-size-fits-most solutions in my view.

All told, this revisit to PLTR is triggered by the release of its Q3 ER and also the latest earnings revisions for Q4. The company kept demonstrating rapid growth, which makes the rule of 40 a very relevant framework to understand its growth, profitability, and valuation. In a nutshell, I see strong potential for the company to maintain or even further expand its triple-digit R40 score. Such a superb score provides a counterbalance to its extreme valuation multiples, leading to my Hold rating.

data.title: Cognex: Fairly Valued Given Limited Near-Term Visibility
data.description:
Cognex (CGNX) achieved solid growth and improved margins in the third quarter. Its business was boosted by one-off revenue, though, and hence this strength shouldn’t be extrapolated. Looking through temporary factors, Cognex's revenue is growing at a single-digit rate, and margins remain depressed due to growth investments.

I previously suggested that caution was warranted due to macro uncertainty, and Cognex's stock is roughly flat since then. While automation and AI adoption support Cognex’s long-term outlook, I continue to believe the stock is fully valued given near-term risks. I am cautious regarding the macro environment, particularly in relation to the large investments that have been made in logistics in recent years.

Market Conditions

While Cognex's revenue is rebounding, supported by acquisitions and partnership revenue, the company continues to face a muted demand environment that varies significantly across end markets.

Consumer electronics, logistics, and packaging were areas of strength in the third quarter. Consumer electronics has been aided by supply chain diversification across geographies, although this will only be a temporary tailwind. Cognex expects its consumer electronics business to return to YoY growth in 2025 for the first time since 2022.

The logistics business continues to grow at a double-digit pace, driven by large e-commerce customers. I question how long this strength will persist, though, due to pressure on consumer spending and the large investments in capacity that have been made in recent years. Cognex has stated that customer spend is primarily related to the automation of existing facilities rather than capacity expansion, though, and also believes that penetration is still low. Packaging is also underpenetrated and is a less cyclical market. Cognex’s new customer initiative is helping to drive adoption in this vertical.

Cognex’s semiconductor business is predominantly vision systems for large equipment manufacturers. This remains a somewhat variable part of the market despite the large investments being made in AI at the moment.

Automotive remains a weak spot, although Cognex believes the decline in 2025 will be less severe than 2024’s 14% contraction. Demand is variable across geographies, with America strong relative to Europe.

It is worthwhile keeping in mind that Cognex has a short-cycle business and hence has limited visibility into future demand conditions. The number of Cognex job openings has been trending down in recent months, which suggests that conditions remain challenging.

Cognex Business Updates

Cognex offers AI-enabled solutions for industrial machine vision applications, positioning the company to capitalize on rising adoption of automation and the rapidly improving capabilities of AI. While this is a large and growing opportunity, it is likely to play out over an extended period, meaning investors should not expect Cognex's growth to suddenly surge. This partially relates to the number and diversity of customers, which complicates the sales process.

Cognex's emerging customer initiative aims to double the company's customer count by driving adoption among smaller organizations. Supporting this, Cognex continues to invest in solutions that are easier to use. Little information has been given on the progress of this initiative in recent quarters.

Cognex recently introduced the SLX (Solutions Experience) product line, which is targeted at logistics. This is a portfolio of AI-enabled machine vision devices that are easy to use. Initial applications targeted by Cognex include object classification and side-by-side detection, which is useful for sorting items. This extends Cognex’s business beyond barcode reading into higher-value applications.

Cognex also recently launched OneVision, a cloud platform that helps manufacturers build and deploy AI-enabled vision tools. OneVision is still in limited release, but feedback from early adopters has been positive. It is hoped that OneVision will drive adoption and unlock new use cases.

Financial Analysis

Cognex generated $277 million in revenue in the third quarter, an increase of 18% YoY. The company benefited from a commercial partnership in Q3 that temporarily boosted revenue ($30 million), although the 2024 comparable period was difficult due to the inclusion of an extra month of revenue from Moritex ($5 million in revenue in Q3 2024). The partnership is targeting medical lab automation and was only expected to provide an $8-$14 million revenue boost in the quarter. Revenue growth excluding these impacts was 13% on a constant currency basis.

Greater China (includes Taiwan) revenue increased 9% YoY even though shifts in ordering entities and the extra month of Moritex revenue were headwinds. Strength was broad-based strength, excluding automotive, and represents a sharp shift from prior quarters. Cognex has suggested that competition has eased and that pricing is stabilizing as a result. Cognex believes its China business is supported by the investments it has made in local capabilities. It is worth noting that around three-quarters of its revenue is from multinationals operating in China rather than domestic manufacturers. Other Asia revenue increased 4% YoY in Q3, adjusting for the additional month of Moritex revenue last year, supported by the relocation of consumer electronics supply chains.

Cognex expects $230-$245 million in revenue in the fourth quarter, representing roughly 3% YoY growth at the midpoint. The company believes it is in the initial stages of an expansion cycle, although this isn't really clear. Manufacturing PMIs suggest stabilization rather than expansion at this stage, and Cognex has limited forward visibility. Customer CapEx could also easily drop from current levels if economic conditions deteriorate.

Cognex's gross margins remain under pressure due to a combination of tariffs and revenue mix. In particular, Moritex and Cognex's logistics businesses have been growth drivers recently and have lower margins. Cognex's gross margin was also hit by tariffs in the third quarter.

Cognex's adjusted EBITDA improved significantly due in large part to the high-margin revenue from the commercial partnership, along with continued cost discipline (adjusted operating expenses were only up 1% YoY). Incentive compensation was a headwind in the quarter, though. Cognex's free cash flows also continue to improve, reaching $214 million over the past 12 months.

Cognex returned $37 million to shareholders in Q3 through a combination of share repurchases and dividends. The company ended the quarter with $600 million in net cash and investments, some of which seems destined for further M&A.

Conclusion

Cognex's revenue multiple is now a little over 6x. While this may seem high for a company that is only expecting a 10% market CAGR through the cycle, there is potential for market share gains, supported by M&A. Cognex also has a demonstrated ability to generate high margins. It will likely take a number of years, but I expect the company's operating margins to eventually exceed 25% again. As a result, I think an argument can be made that the stock will provide mid- to low-teen returns over the long run.

There is also the issue of the current demand environment to consider. I see no reason to expect Automotive demand to rebound in the short term. Consumer electronics demand could also slip again once supply chain relocation efforts are finished. Cognex's business really hinges on large ongoing investments in logistics. Given the faltering state of the economy, I wouldn't rely on this part of the business continuing to generate strong growth. As a result, I am "Neutral" on Cognex's stock currently, even though the company has a bright future.
