data.title: Strategy Dilutes Its Equity As Bitcoin Crashes: A Major Red Flag
data.description:
Strategy Inc (MSTR) stock is in a confirmed bear market this year, having fallen 37% in price, while the S&P 500 rose 15.5%. The main cause of the selloff was obvious enough: Bitcoin (BTC-USD) fell in price, while making up over 90% of Strategy’s balance sheet assets. It’s understandable that a stock would come down in price after the asset it holds comes down in price; in Strategy’s case, the passively owned asset is worth orders of magnitude more than the entire operating business. Strategy has further borrowed extensively in order to acquire its Bitcoin position. It’s for this reason that many investors see Strategy as a Bitcoin proxy, akin to a leveraged BTC ETF.

So, the main reason for Strategy’s poor performance this year is obvious enough. However, there are other things under the hood too. In addition to having bought up an asset that has performed poorly this year, Strategy has also diluted its equity extensively. At the end of 2024, Strategy had 192.5 million shares outstanding. As of this year’s most recent report, the company had 283.5 million shares outstanding. So, in under a year, we have seen Strategy increase its share count by 47%, reducing each shareholder’s equity interest to a considerable degree. Bitcoin would need to rise at least 47% in price to overcome the effect of this dilution; in light of this, it’s somewhat surprising that MSTR is down just 38% this year.

Dilution is not something new for Strategy. In dilution to having diluted its equity this year, Strategy has done the same over the last five years, nearly tripling its share count in that period. At the end of 2020, the company had 96.8 million shares outstanding, which rose to 283.5 million by last quarter. So, the company has increased its share count by 194.7% over five years.

The dilution issue doesn’t stop there, either. Strategy has financed its Bitcoin purchases largely with convertible debt, a type of debt that offers holders an option to exchange their shares for equity, in exchange for them receiving a lower interest rate. As a result of using this type of debt, Strategy has somewhat lower interest expenses than you’d think, given its amount of debt ($70.4 million in interest on $8.2 billion worth of debt). The downside of this arrangement is that debt holders have the right to exchange their bonds for equity, so there is even more dilution potentially coming in the future.

Put simply, Strategy is an unsound financial house that can only keep itself going if Bitcoin continues rising in price extremely rapidly. This year, with Bitcoin having fallen 1.8% in price, "rapid price appreciation" is the furthest thing from what's actually being observed. When I started writing this article, Bitcoin was down 8% for the year, but it regained lost ground in an overnight rally. If BTC had remained down 8% then the dilution would have been a major issue; it will become a major issue again if BTC once again starts falling. It is already an issue now, from a value perspective: Bitcoin has not risen anywhere near 47% in value since MSTR equity was diluted by 47%; in fact, it has fallen in value slightly. This is the reason for MSTR’s poor stock price performance.

However, if Bitcoin starts really crashing, like it did in 2018, then actual threats to MSTR’s financial soundness could emerge. Strategy has $70.4 million in annual interest expenses and a free cash flow negative operating business. The company's Bitcoin holdings are estimated to be worth $55.9 billion. If Bitcoin crashes 90%, these holdings fall to $5.59 billion in value, and suddenly $70.4 million in interest expenses starts looking like a big deal. In such a scenario, the company has to sell of 1.2% of its Bitcoin each year to pay interest, and that's not to mention other operating expenses. The end result could be the company chipping away at its assets while its debts continue having to be paid. That combined with dilution could deliver a truly dismal result to shareholders.

When I last covered Strategy, I rated it a sell on the grounds that it was exposed to Bitcoin’s volatility in a highly leveraged package, while having none of Bitcoin’s practical use cases. I still stand by the essence of what I wrote in that article, which was a success, as MSTR fell 45% in price after it published. Now, however, we have MSTR trading at a much lower price than it did when I last wrote about it. Those who shorted the stock when my sell rating was published, now have an excellent opportunity to take profits. While I’d still rather sell MSTR than own it, I think the easy money has been made shorting MSTR. Accordingly, I’m upgrading my rating on the stock to hold. In the ensuing paragraphs, I will explain why I made that decision.

The Bear Case On MSTR

A bear case on MSTR can be built on its leveraged exposure to Bitcoin, heavy dilution, and high level of convertible debt.

First, let’s take a look at Strategy’s Bitcoin exposure. It’s questionable why a corporation would choose to serve as a proxy for a cryptocurrency. Unlike an ETF, a company has to pay taxes whenever it earns profit selling Bitcoin. Currently, Strategy’s position is that it won’t sell its Bitcoin, but it does not earn enough money from its operating business to pay its interest (at least not most years). So, it may be forced to sell its Bitcoin in order to meet obligations. Currently, its approach is to borrow more money in order to service the interest on previous borrowings. This is likely unsustainable–if I were a gambling man, I’d bet on Strategy being forced to sell Bitcoin sooner rather than later.

Now, if Strategy ever is forced to sell its Bitcoin, its disadvantage compared to a Bitcoin ETF will become evident: the company will have to pay taxes. Potentially, large ones: short term capital gains are taxed at 21% federally. Additionally, when you play Bitcoin through MSTR, you forego the practical use cases of Bitcoin; i.e. spending it and using it for remittances. Given its disadvantages compared to both ETFs and direct Bitcoin holdings, it’s not clear what the “purpose” of holding Strategy as a Bitcoin proxy is.

Next up, we have Strategy’s heavy amounts of leverage. According to Seeking Alpha Quant, MSTR has $8.2 billion worth of debt, incurring $70.4 million in annual interest expenses. The interest rate implied by these figures is 0.85%, which is rather low. However, there are issues with the way that MSTR achieves its low-interest rate. It does so by issuing convertible debt; i.e., debt that can be converted into MSTR stock. Such debts usually have lower interest rates because the conversion option is perceived as being valuable. However, they create even more dilution if bondholders choose to exercise their options. As I showed previously, Bitcoin needs to rise at least 47% to make up for the dilution in Strategy stock observed in the last year. If more bondholders convert their bonds, then MSTR will have to rise more than 47%. That’s not the kind of thing that a risk-averse investor tends to like betting on.

Last but not least, there’s the multiples that Strategy trades at. The company has negative cash flows, so many valuation multiples are unavailable to us. However, it scores extremely poorly in terms of EV/sales, price/sales and EV/EBITDA. These “EV” multiples are ones that take into account Strategy’s debt, indicating that the stock is expensive when factoring in the claims by all financial interests in the underlying company.

Why I Wouldn't Go Short Here

As you can likely tell by the previous paragraphs, I take a fairly dim view of Strategy’s financial prospects. Indeed, I would rather short Strategy than buy it, if someone put a gun to my head and forced me to choose. But in reality, I think that at this point, MSTR stock is better off avoided than shorted. I’d advise anybody who shorted Strategy around the time my previous article went live, to take profits now.

Why?

There are a few reasons. First, Bitcoin is currently near the lower end of its 52-week range. Going by history, the expectation would be for the cryptocurrency to recover from here, not fall further. That’s no guarantee; it’s just what price momentum from the recent past would predict. Bitcoin certainly could fall further from here, but I have no reason to think that it will.

Second, my previous Strategy call (a sell call on September of 2025) was quite successful, and anybody who traded based on it (i.e. shorted Strategy) and did not sell until today, would be sitting on a large, market beating gain. It would be prudent to take those profits, given the unpredictable nature of Bitcoin price fluctuations.

Third and finally, Strategy does have some things going for it, such as the ability to obtain debt financing ‘cheaply’ (considering interest rates alone). This financing isn't cheap from the perspective of shareholders, who face more dilution from the convertible bonds; but it is cheap from management's perspective, as it does not require them to pay significant interest. So the company can probably keep kicking the can down the road for a surprisingly long time.

The Bottom Line

Taking everything covered in this article into account, I think that Strategy is a stock best avoided. Having come down in price by nearly 50%, it’s no longer the obvious short it once was. But it’s not a buy either. Investors considering any position in MSTR would be better off thinking about other opportunities.
